coredata.dc:title: Dual utilization of aloe peel: Aloe peel-derived carbon quantum dots enhanced anaerobic co-digestion of aloe peel
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lijianan
Yun, Sining
Ke, Teng
Wang, Kaijun
An, Jinhang
Liu, Jiayu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon materials have been widely used in anaerobic digestion (AD), but the role of zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in anaerobic co-digestion (AcoD) has not yet been reported. In this work, the effect of aloe peel-derived CQDs (AP-CQDs) on the AcoD system of aloe peel and dairy manure was investigated. The addition of AP-CQDs accelerants increased the cumulative CH4 yield from 201.14 to 266.92–339.64 mL/g VS and increased total chemical oxygen demand removal efficiency from 34.72 % to 48.77–57.87 %. The use of a digestate with 0.36 wt.% of AP-CQDs resulted in a thermogravimetric mass loss of 47.15 % and a promising total nutrient content of 46.65 g/kg. The excellent electron exchange capacity of AP-CQDs may facilitate direct interspecies electron transfer during the AD process. Moreover, the use of AP-CQDs can enrich methanogenic microorganisms (Methanosarcina and Methanobacterium). These findings provide a viable strategy for improving methane production and create awareness regarding the dual use of biomass waste.

head.ce:title: Dual utilization of aloe peel: Aloe peel-derived carbon quantum dots enhanced anaerobic co-digestion of aloe peel

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon materials have been widely used in anaerobic digestion (AD), but the role of zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in anaerobic co-digestion (AcoD) has not yet been reported. In this work, the effect of aloe peel-derived CQDs (AP-CQDs) on the AcoD system of aloe peel and dairy manure was investigated. The addition of AP-CQDs accelerants increased the cumulative CH4 yield from 201.14 to 266.92–339.64 mL/g VS and increased total chemical oxygen demand removal efficiency from 34.72 % to 48.77–57.87 %. The use of a digestate with 0.36 wt.% of AP-CQDs resulted in a thermogravimetric mass loss of 47.15 % and a promising total nutrient content of 46.65 g/kg. The excellent electron exchange capacity of AP-CQDs may facilitate direct interspecies electron transfer during the AD process. Moreover, the use of AP-CQDs can enrich methanogenic microorganisms (Methanosarcina and Methanobacterium). These findings provide a viable strategy for improving methane production and create awareness regarding the dual use of biomass waste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aloe is cultivated for numerous applications in medical, food, and cosmetic fields (Baruah et al., 2016). Owing to its intensive use, a substantial quantity of aloe peel (AP) waste is created, which contains lignin, carbohydrates, and proteins (Dahiya et al., 2018). Similarly, livestock farming produces a large amount of animal manure. Animal manure contains pathogens, hormones, and other harmful substances, which may contaminate surface water and groundwater resources (Hu et al., 2017). In addition, greenhouse gases, ammonia, and other gases produced by animal manure are released into the air, contributing to climate change and the formation of harmful particulates (Herrero et al., 2016). These wastes should be post-processed using the principles of circularization to achieve sustainable use of waste and reduce the burden that waste disposal has on the environment.

Anaerobic digestion (AD) is a mature technology that allows for the efficient and economical disposal of biomass waste, reduces the environmental impact of biomass waste, decreases the risk of disease in humans, and generates renewable energy (CH4) to mitigate energy-shortage problems (Li et al., 2019a). The resulting digestate can be used as a bio-organic fertilizer (Han et al., 2019). However, AD has disadvantages, such as the long duration of the digestion cycles, the low degradation efficiency of organic matter, and poor stability of the digestate. In addition, the amount of nitrogen in the digestate poses a risk to the environment (Abbas et al., 2021a; Namburath et al., 2020). Effective methods against these drawbacks include pretreatment of AD substrates (Bianco et al., 2021), co-digestion (Li et al., 2021a), process control of AD (Li et al., 2021b), and supplementation of accelerants (Yun et al., 2021). Co-digestion and supplementation of accelerants have been extensively studied. Anaerobic co-digestion (AcoD) is the simultaneous digestion of two or more substrates, it is characterized by nutritional balance, a higher number of metals and trace elements, and an enhanced buffering capacity. Compared to the anaerobic mono-digestion of the same substrate, the AcoD can enhance biogas yields by 25–400 % (Hagos et al., 2017).

To date, carbon accelerants (e.g., biochar, bio-based carbon, graphene, graphite felt, and carbon nanotubes) have been used to enhance the overall performance of AD systems by shortening the lag period, increasing methane productivity, and improving microbial activity (Abbas et al., 2021a). For instance, the AD reactor with 1.5 g/L of multi-walled carbon nanotubes had a 12.6 % higher cumulative CH4 yield than that of a control reactor, and the multi-walled carbon nanotubes interacted with extracellular polymers to protect the cells from external influences (Ambuchi et al., 2017). Compared to that with the control group, adding graphite felt into the AD reactor with propionate and ethanol as the substrate increased the CH4 production rate by 19.1 %. This occurred owing to the high specific surface area and electrical conductivity of graphite felt, which were conducive to the degradation of the substrate and the growth and metabolism of anaerobic microorganisms (Zhang et al., 2019). The addition of 15 g/L of different biochar to the reactants reduced the lag period from 15 to 1.1–3.0 days and increased the maximum methane production rate from 4.0 to 10.4–13.9 mL/day compared with the those of the control group, contributing to the abundant functional groups of biochar, which promoted electron transfer between electro-active microbes (Wang et al., 2020a). Bio-based carbon used in the AcoD system coupled with microbial electrolysis cells increased the biogas and CH4 yield by 38.62 % and 34.26 %, respectively, compared with those of the control group, and the improvement was attributed to the high electron exchange capacity of bio-based carbon facilitating the direct interspecific electron transfer (DIET) between fermentative bacteria and methanogens (Xing et al., 2021). Furthermore, Li et al. (2021b) added graphene (10 g/L) to the AcoD system of coal gasification wastewater, which increased the CH4 production rate from 450 to 500 mL/day and chemical oxygen demand (COD) removal rate from 9.93 % to 70 %, mainly because the graphene alleviated the inhibition of coenzyme F420 by the total phenols and increased the concentration of coenzyme F420 by 53.33 %. A high concentration of coenzyme F420 can assist in the conversion of acetate to methane (Li et al., 2021b). As shown by the results of previous works (Abbas et al., 2021a; Li et al., 2021b), carbon-based materials have high electrical conductivity, abundant functional groups, and large surface area, which are beneficial characteristics for the immobilization and enrichment of anaerobic microorganisms, and DIET during AD, thus promoting biogas production and organics matter degradation.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional carbon-based material with a particle size of<10 nm, have recently attracted considerable attention owing to their low manufacturing cost, low toxicity, and high stability (Li et al., 2021c). Compared with other carbon materials (biochar, bio-based carbon, and activated carbon), CQDs are richer in hydrophilic carbonyl and carboxyl groups, which uniformly disperse in water and form increased contact with the substrate during the reaction, thereby increasing the reaction rate of AD (Rani et al., 2020). In addition, CQDs exhibit superior electron-accepting and electron-donating properties (Liu et al., 2019), showing their promise as accelerants for AD. More importantly, no researchers have used biomass derived CQDs as accelerants in AcoD systems, and the potential role of CQDs in promoting methanogenesis during AcoD process is still unclear.

This work investigated the effect of aloe peel-derived CQDs (AP-CQDs) on AcoD performance and further clarified the underlying mechanism of enhanced methanogenesis by AP-CQDs. The structure and properties of the AP-CQDs were first examined to characterize them. Then, the feasibility of AP-CQDs as accelerants was evaluated by the AcoD system performance, including biogas yield and composition, pH, total COD (TCOD) removal efficiency, volatile and total solids (VS, TS) removal rate, as well as the fertility, thermal stability, and electrochemical characterization of the digestate. Finally, a general strategy for understanding the performance enhancement of the AcoD system by AP-CQDs accelerants was proposed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Obtainment and collection of substrate and inoculum
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, dairy manure (DM) and AP were used as co-substrates and anaerobic sewage sludge (ASS) as inoculum. DM contains feces and urine was collected at farming in Lantian county of Xi’an, China. The AP was collected from an aloe beverage factory in the Yangling county of Xi’an. To increase the degradability of AP, the alkaline pretreatment has been used to break the surface structure of the AP and destroy the ester bonds of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin; according to the optimal pretreatment conditions previously obtained by our research group and others (Girotto et al., 2018; Xing et al., 2021), the fragments were soaked in 2 M NaOH solution for 1 day at room temperature and then washed with water to neutral. The ASS came from a sewage treatment company in Xi’an. The characteristics of DM, AP, and ASS were listed in Table 1 .

The AP-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of AP (Kumar et al., 2017), and the preparation process was shown in Fig. S1 . After drying in an oven, the AP was crushed in a grinder for 3 min and passed through a 50-mesh sieve. The AP powder was obtained.

Subsequently, the AP powder was evenly mixed with deionized (DI) water at 1:125 (g:mL). The 50 mL mixed solution was placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The hydrothermal treatment was conducted at 200 °C for 5 h. To remove large particles the obtained yellow solution was ultracentrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min. The centrifugation step was repeated twice. The centrifugal liquid was filtered using a membrane of a specific size (0.22 μm). The obtained solution containing AP-CQDs was dialyzed against DI water using dialysis membrane (Molecular Weight Cut-off: 1000 Da) for 48 h (Alam et al., 2015; Wareing et al., 2021). The purified AP-CQDs solution was dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 48 h. Finally, the dried AP-CQDs were stored at 4 °C for further characterization and use.

Based on the findings of previous works, the co-substrate and inoculum were added to the reactors in a weight ratio of 3:9:28 (DM:AP:ASS) to maintain optimal TS content (Ke et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2018). After homogeneous mixing, the TS and VS contents, pH, and C/N of the bioreactor were 7.10 %, 5.06 %, 7.15, and 15.67, respectively, as summarized in Table 1. A total of five experimental groups were set up, including four groups with AP-CQDs (0.09, 0.18, 0.27, and 0.36 wt.%) and one group as the CK group without AP-CQDs. Each reactor was shaken manually for 2 min once a day during the AcoD process. The experiments were repeated three times.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of AP-CQDs accelerants were obtained by an automated X-ray diffractometer (D/Max2200, America). The surface functional groups of AP-CQDs accelerants were investigated by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrometer (Thermo Fisher IS50, America). The morphology, distribution, and dimensions of the AP-CQDs accelerator were observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Thermo Scientific Talos F200X, America). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded at room temperature on a spectrophotometer (Youke TN600, China) to observe the absorption peaks of the AP-CQDs accelerants. The daily biogas yield was obtained by draining water gathering of gas law, and the results were the average values of three experiments. Biogas was finally expressed as in units of mL/g VS (Gas volumes were normalized to standard temperature and pressure and expressed as NmL/g VS), where VS refers to VS of the AcoD system. Biogas (CH4, CO2, and H2) compositions were analyzed by gas chromatography (GC9790, China). TS, VS and TCOD concentrations were measured in accordance with the standard methods (APHA, 2005). As for the sampling of liquid phase, we measured the initial and final TS, VS and TCOD to calculate the removal efficiency, so sampling was performed at the beginning and end of batch experiments. Changes in pH during AcoD systems were measured using a pH meter (PHS-3C, China). The thermogravimetric (TG), differential thermogravimetry (DTG), and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curves of the digestate were obtained using a simultaneous thermal analyzer (STA449F3, Germany). The total nitrogen (TN) was measured by the auto Kjeldahl analyzer (JK9830, China). The total phosphorus (TP) was measured by UV Spectrophotometer (Rayleigh VIS-723 N, China). The total potassium (TK) was measured by flame atomic absorption spectrometry (AA-7003, China).

Electrochemical linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and mediated electrochemical reduction/oxidation (MER/MEO) tests were carried out using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, China). The CV, LSV, MER and MEO tests were performed using a three-electrode structure, with a graphite rod as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and a graphite rod (CV and LSV tests) or glassy carbon (MER and MEO tests) as the working electrode. The digestate of the AcoD was used as the electrolyte for LSV and CV tests, and the scan rate was 50 mV/s. The MEO and MER tests were performed in buffer solutions (0.1 M phosphate, 0.1 M KCl, pH=7) using diquat dibromide monohydrate and 2,2′-amino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt as electron shuttling agents, respectively. Other details are referred to the published literature (Xing et al., 2021). Electron accepting/donating capacity (EAC/EDC) were calculated according to the following formulae:

As a widely used model, modified Gompertz model with high correlation coefficient when fitting experimental data of biogas yield was used to determine the relevant kinetic parameters (Bianco et al., 2022; Oliva et al., 2022). Expression for the model was given.

where M(t) is the cumulative biogas yields (mL/g VS) on day t, M is the cumulative biogas yields potential (mL/g VS), Rm is the maximum biogas yields rate (mL/g VS/d), λ is the lag phase (d), t is the digestion time (d), and e is the constant 2.71828.

During the AcoD process, the CH4 yield and TCOD removal efficiency of the group with 0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs were the highest. Therefore, to clarify the representative changes in the microbial community, high-throughput sequencing was performed on the CK group and the group with the optimal dosage of AP-CQDs (0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs). Briefly, DNA was extracted from the digestate using an OMEGA E.Z.N.ATM Mag-Bind Soil DNA Kit. Extracted DNA was used for amplification and sequencing. The bacterial amplification primers were 341F:5′-CCTACGGGNGGCWGCAG-3′ and 805R:5′-GACTACHVGGGTATCTAATCC-3′, and the archaeal amplification primers were 349F:5′-GYGCASCAGKCGMGAAW-3′ and 806R:5′-GGACTACVSGGGTATCTAAT-3′. The results were analyzed by https://www.arb-silva.de/. The sequence data reported in this work were archived in the Sequence Read Archive (SRA) under the accession number PRJNA890379 (SRP402806).
The statistical analysis was performed by one-way analysis of variation using SPSS and p < 0.05 was considered a statistically significant difference.

The XRD patterns, FTIR spectrum, UV–vis absorption spectrum, and TEM images of the as-prepared AP-CQDs are shown in the supplementary information . There was a distinct peak around 22.0° as shown in the XRD pattern (Fig. S3 a), attributed to the (002) crystal face of graphite. This indicates that AP-CQDs are amorphous and mainly composed of carbon (Kumar et al., 2017). The results showed (Fig. S3 b) that the characteristic absorption bands formed were caused by the stretching vibrations of –OH (3420 and 1086 cm−1), C–H (2923 cm−1) and COO– (1595 and 1398 cm−1) (Lu et al., 2012). The observed surface functional groups on the surface of AP-CQDs based on the FTIR spectrum are similar to those reported in the previous work (Xu et al., 2015).

A shoulder peak in the UV–vis absorption spectrum ( Fig. S4 ) at 278 nm is thought to be the π − π* transition of C=C and the n − π* transition of C=O (Yang et al., 2014). These functional groups can be electron donors and electron acceptors, which may have a positive effect on the DIET process (Li et al., 2021a; Li et al., 2021d). The aqueous solution of AP-CQDs in the inset of Fig. S4 was transparent yellow in visible light but turns intensely blue under UV (365 nm) excitation. TEM image ( Fig. S5 ) revealed that AP-CQDs derived from AP were found to be 3–8 nm in diameter with a narrow size distribution, mono-dispersed, and spherical morphology. Based on the above observations, it is clear that AP-CQDs have been successfully achieved.

Fig. 1
a shows the reduction and oxidation current peaks generated by increasing the amounts of AP-CQDs added to the MER and MEO electrochemical cells. The high reduction and oxidation currents reflect the high electron transfer rates of the AP-CQDs (Xing et al., 2021). The integration of the current peaks produces the number of electrons transferred (Q). As shown in Fig. 1 b, Q was positively correlated with the promoter mass with correlation coefficients R2 of 0.93 and 0.97, respectively. The slope of the linear regression line of Q versus the mass of the added AP-CQDs corresponds to the EAC and EDC. The EAC and EDC values for the AP-CQDs were 0.36 ± 0.06 μmol e- (g AP-CQDs)-1 and 0.34 ± 0.03 μmol e- (g AP-CQDs)-1, respectively. Electron exchange capacity (EEC = EAC + EDC) describes the total capacity of a material to accept and donate electrons. EEC is related to the surface functional groups of the accelerants, where quinone (C=O) can enhance the EAC (Li et al., 2021a). As shown in Section 3.1 , the AP-CQDs accelerants had a larger variety of functional groups on their surfaces, making them capable of inducing a better EEC (0.70 μmol e- (g AP-CQDs)-1). The EEC of bio-based carbon prepared by Li et al. (2021a) and Xing et al. (2021) were 0.31–0.66 μmol e- (g bio-based carbon)-1. This also indicates that AP-CQDs have better EEC than aforementioned carbon materials. These results suggest that the introduction of AP-CQDs can facilitate the EEC of the AcoD system. The enhancement of EEC can facilitate the electron transfer between microorganisms, thereby promoting DIET. This has been confirmed in the previous work (Li et al., 2021a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of AP-CQDs accelerants on the AcoD performance
Effect of AP-CQDs addition on biogas yields
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2
a shows the daily biogas yields for all AcoD systems. Obviously, two distinct peaks can be observed. The first peak may be caused by carbohydrate digestion and the second one may be caused by crude protein digestion, similar to previous works (Abbas et al., 2021b; Wang et al., 2020b). Compared with the CK group, the first peak and the second peak of digestors with AP-CQDs accelerants were 1–2 and 1–5 days earlier, respectively. Similar phenomena have been found in previous published studies (Li et al., 2021a; Yun et al., 2018). The maximum daily biogas yield (41.53 mL/g VS/d; 38.50 NmL/g VS/d) with the addition of 0.18 wt.% AP-CQDs accelerants, which was significantly (p = 0.006) higher than that of the CK group (37.33 mL/g VS/d; 34.20 NmL/g VS/d). These outcomes indicated that the added AP-CQDs accelerants played a good role in the AcoD system in shortening the digestion cycle and increasing biogas yield.

The cumulative biogas yields measurements and predictions for the different groups are shown in Fig. 2 b, with the relevant parameters shown in Table S1 and Table S2 . The cumulative biogas yields increase as the concentration of AP-CQDs accelerants increases in the range of 0–0.36 wt.%, and the maximum value of 570.86 mL/g VS (522.96 NmL/g VS) was achieved when the content of AP-CQDs was 0.36 wt.%. Compared with the CK, the cumulative biogas yield of the reactor with 0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs was increased by 15.84 %. In addition, the cumulative biogas production of all additive groups was statistically analyzed with respect to the CK group, only the addition of 0.09 wt.% did not significantly (p = 0.109) increased, and the rest showed a significant (p < 0.05) increase. The cumulative methane production of all the groups was in precise agreement with the modified Gompertz model, and the correlation coefficients were above 0.98. The M of the 0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs group was 571.41 mL/g VS (523.50 NmL/g VS), and its Rm reached 30.83 mL/g VS/d (28.24 NmL/g VS/d). This indicates that AP-CQDs can enhance biogas yields.

During AcoD, the generated biogas is collected using gas collection bags every-five days and analyzed further for gas composition. The cumulative biogas production is multiplied by the average CH4 and CO2 content to determine the CH4 and CO2 yields (Fig. 2 c and Table S3 ), respectively. The CH4 yields obtained from the AcoD system were 201.14 mL/g VS for CK, 266.92 mL/g VS for 0.09 wt.% AP-CQDs, 286.07 mL/g VS for 0.18 wt.% AP-CQDs, 304.31 mL/g VS for 0.27 wt.% AP-CQDs, and 339.64 mL/g VS for 0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs. The CO2 yields obtained from the AcoD system were 108.05 mL/g VS for CK, 152.55 mL/g VS for 0.09 wt.% AP-CQDs, 99.74 mL/g VS for 0.18 wt.% AP-CQDs, 132.07 mL/g VS for 0.27 wt.% AP-CQDs, and 115.62 mL/g VS for 0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs. The yields of CH4 and CO2 at standard temperature and pressure are shown in Table S4 . The cumulative methane yield increased by 32.70–68.85 % with the addition of AP-CQDs (0.09–0.36 wt.%) compared with that of CK, which was comparable to or higher than the increase in yield (10.52–77.36 %) of the AD systems with carbon materials as accelerants reported in previous works ( Table S5 ). By contrast, it can be seen from Table S3 that the CH4 content were 50.63 % (0.09 wt.% AP-CQDs), 52.36 % (0.18 wt.% AP-CQDs), 55.22 % (0.27 wt.% AP-CQDs), and 59.50 % (0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs), were higher than that (40.82 %) for CK. In addition, the sum of CH4 and CO2 in the CK group was far away from the 100 %, which may be due to the biogas containing N2, H2, NH3, H2S, and water vapour. Similar observations can be found in the previous papers (Chen et al., 2020b; Shen et al., 2021). In addition, the presence of N2 is the most possible reason for these high differences between the 100 % and the sum of CO2 and CH4. The N2 was externally introduced due to the sparging when the operators opened the reactors. This caused the sum of CO2, CH4, and H2 not be close to 100% in some cases. Usually, the raw biogas includes methane (CH4, 35–70 %), carbon dioxide (CO2, 15–50 %), and other gases (2–8 %) (Li et al., 2021a). These results indicate that the addition of AP-CQDs can indeed improve methane production in the AcoD system. This may be due to the AP-CQDs accelerants enhancing the electron transfer mechanism between acetogens and methanogens (Li et al., 2021d). It should be noted that the carbon quantum dots could play more important role in fermentation with light, which might be more significant than the function like biochar. The mechanism by which AP-CQDs enhance the methanogenic performance of AcoD systems is described in detail in Section 3.5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH values, TCOD removal efficiency, and TS and VS reductions
ce:section.ce:para:
As an important index to evaluate the AcoD environment, the pH values can significantly affect the growth of microorganisms (Wang et al., 2020b). The changes of pH during AcoD are shown in Fig. 3
a. The hydrolysis of organic matter at the beginning of AcoD, producing small-molecules substances and acids, causes a decrease in pH. This is consistent with the previous finding (Li et al., 2019b). The change in pH may not clearly indicate the change in volatile fatty acids content during AcoD, and therefore volatile fatty acids will be measured in the future. During the AcoD process, the pH of all reactors varied between 5.52 and 7.22, which fell within the appropriate range of 5.5–8.2 (Han et al., 2019). Compared with the CK group, the addition of AP-CQDs did not substantially decrease the pH value during the AD process, indicating that the systems containing AP-CQDs additives were able to maintain a stable and smooth digestion process.

The removal efficiency of TCOD is an important indicator to characterize the decomposition of the substrate. As shown in Fig. 3 b, after 35 days of the AcoD process, all TCOD removal efficiency containing AP-CQDs accelerants were significantly (p < 0.05) higher than the CK group. The removal efficiency of TCOD increased with an increase in the concentration of AP-CQDs. The maximum TCOD removal efficiency was 57.87 %. According to previous literature, higher TCOD removal efficiency indicates a more complete AcoD reaction and low environmental impact of the digestate (Wang et al., 2021a). Higher TCOD removal efficiency leads to greater biogas yields. The TCOD removal efficiency indicates that the addition of AP-CQDs promotes the decomposition of substrates.

The TS and VS concentrations of all reactors are listed in Table S6 . The TS and VS removal rates were significantly (p < 0.05) higher in the AcoD system with AP-CQDs accelerants than those in the CK group. When the optimal concentration of AP-CQDs was 0.36 wt.%, the removal rates of TS and VS in the AcoD system were 53.45 % and 47.41 %, respectively, 16.78 % and 14.13 % higher than those of CK. This indicates a significant increase in the biodegradation rate associated with the addition of AP-CQDs.

Thermal analysis is a common method for assessing the stability of the digestates (Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). In this work, TG, DTG, and DSC curves obtained from simultaneous thermal analysis are used to assess the stability of the digestate. The mass loss of the digestate sample increases with increasing temperature. These mass losses are mainly due to the pyrolysis of organic material (Wang et al., 2022). The TG curve is divided into three stages (Fig. 4
a). In the first stage (50–200 °C), the loss of mass is mainly due to the loss of water (Wang et al., 2022). In the second stage (200–400 °C), the mass loss is mainly due to the pyrolysis of simple organic matter (Jia et al., 2020). In the third stage (400–700 °C) the mass loss is due to the pyrolysis of refractory organic materials (possibly lignin-based materials) (Wang et al., 2019a). The total mass loss of the tested samples (Fig. 4 b) decreased with increase amounts of AP-CQDs accelerants. The lower the total mass loss, the more completely the substrate is digested and the higher the thermal stability of the digestate (Wang et al., 2022; Xing et al., 2021). The total mass loss of the digestate containing different concentrations of AP-CQDs was 0.09 wt.% (51.32 %), 0.18 wt.% (50.57 %), 0.27 wt.% (49.61 %), and 0.36 wt.% (47.15 %), all of which were lower than that of the CK group (52.91 %). This indicated that the digestates were more stable when accelerants were added.

Four peaks are observed in the DTG curve (Fig. 4 c), corresponding to the three TG stages: dehydration (peak around 100 °C), pyrolysis of short-chain organic substances (peak around 290 °C), and pyrolysis of complex organic matter (peaks around 490 °C and 640 °C) (Xing et al., 2021). The higher the peak, the more organic matter is left undigested (Xing et al., 2021). The peak of the digestates containing AP-CQDs was lower than of the CK digestates, indicating that the accelerants increased system stability. The DSC curve of the digestates shows two distinct exothermic peaks (Fig. 4 d). Exothermic peaks for carbohydrates, hemicelluloses, etc., are observed at around 300 °C. The exothermic peak for lignocellulose is observed at about 500 °C (Wang et al., 2021a). The lower the peak value, the more complete the organic matter is digested (Wang et al., 2021a). The introduction of the AP-CQDs accelerants resulted in a lower intensity of the two peaks in the DSC curve compared with those of the control, indicating that the addition of AP-CQDs enhanced the biodegradation of organic matter in the substrate. This also corresponded to the mass loss in the TG curve. The results of TG, DTG, and DSC suggested that the addition of AP-CQDs accelerants can increase the stability of the digestates by promoting the decomposition and conversion of organic matter into biogas. The change trend of these results was consistent with the change in biogas production, TS, VS, and TCOD.

The digestate produced after AD contains nutrients such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium required by the crop, which can be used as a biofertilizer or one component of the compound fertilizer to achieve the recycling of resources (Ke et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2020). The TN, TP, and TK data for this work (0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs) and similar work are shown in Table 2 . According to the current standard set (NY/T 525–2021), the mass fraction of total nutrients (N + P2O5 + K2O) in bio-organic fertilizers should exceed 4.0 % (Wang et al., 2022). The reasonable range of N application in agriculture is 67.66–470.57 kg N/ha (Qin et al., 2021). The total nutrient content of the digestates was 4.67 %, indicating that the digestate had a greater potential for use as an organic or compound fertilizer. The TN content of the digestates with AP-CQDs added was 21.74 g/kg, which was comparable or superior to that of digestates with bio-based carbon reported in previous works (Chen et al., 2020a; Xing et al., 2021). However, TP and TK content of digestates were low. This indicates that digestates need to be combined with phosphorus and potassium enriched fertilizers to ensure a balanced increase in soil fertility (Li et al., 2021a). Nutrients in digestates can help sustain soil fertility and make the soil more conducive to crop growth (Han et al., 2019). In general, the digestate from AcoD systems with the addition of optimal concentrations of AP-CQDs is considerably fertile and has the potential to be used as a component of compound bio-organic fertilizer, reducing the consumption of chemical fertilizers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Understanding for the enhanced methanogenesis in AcoD system with AP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In AD systems, methanogenic substrates are produced from complex organic matter by hydrolysis, acidogenesis, and acetogenesis (Fig. 5
a). Typically, complex organic matter is microbially broken down by hydrolysis into soluble substrates (proteins, polysaccharides, and lipids), which are further utilized to produce volatile fatty acids (VFAs) and alcohols (Xiao et al., 2021). During acidogenesis, acetogens oxidize alcohols and VFAs into CH3COOH, H2, and CO2. During methanogenesis, the methanogens convert different intermediates (CH3COOH, CO2, and H2) into methane. For instance, acetate can be metabolized by acetotrophic methanogens to produce methane: CH3COO– + H+ → CH4 + CO2; hydrogenotrophic methanogens consume H2 and CO2 to produce methane: 4H2 + CO2 → CH4 + 2H2O, which is also known as interspecies hydrogen transfer (IHT); and direct interspecies electron transfer CO2 (DIET-CO2) reduction is the direct reduction of CO2 by methanogens to produce methane: CO2 + 8H+ + 8e- → CH4 + 2H2O (Xiao et al., 2021).

In AD systems, two-thirds of the methane is produced through the acetotrophic methanogenesis pathway, whereas the rest is produced by IHT and DIET-CO2 reduction (Xiao et al., 2021). Unfortunately, IHT is thermodynamically advantageous only when the H2 partial pressure is sufficiently low (H2 < 10−4 atm) (Li et al., 2019a; Wang et al., 2022). Furthermore, the IHT is limited by the H2 concentration gradient and diffusion rate (Wang et al., 2022). Notably, the DIET-CO2 reduction process involves the direct transfer of electrons from acetogens to methanogens, rather than via H2. This unique electron transfer between acetogens and methanogens makes methane production thermodynamically more efficient (Xiao et al., 2021).

A possible strategy is proposed to understand the role of AP-CQDs accelerants in the AcoD process. In the present work, the AP-CQDs accelerants could establish interbacterial electrical connections (Fig. 5 b) in two possible ways to facilitate the DIET process. In the absence of an exogenous accelerant (Fig. 5 b, Type I), the DIET between acetogens and methanogens is achieved using conductive pili. Pili, which are conductive nanowires 3–5 nm in diameter and 10–20 μm in length, are formed by protein filaments anchored in the cell envelope (Xiao et al., 2021). When AP-CQDs (<10 nm) accelerants are added to the AcoD system, they may attach to conductive pili, acetogens and methanogens. Firstly, when AP-CQDs attach to conductive pili (Fig. 5 b, Type II), the electron transfer ability of the conductive pili was enhanced, thus increasing methane production. This phenomenon has been observed for similarly sized accelerants. Liu et al. (2015) conducted TEM observations on microorganisms with and without added nano-magnetite, and found that nano-magnetite adsorbed on conductive pili of microorganisms enhanced electron transfer between microorganisms, thus accelerating the rate of ethanol metabolism. Secondly, owing to the excellent EEC (0.70 μmol e- (g AP-CQDs)-1) of AP-CQDs (as illustrated in Fig. 1), the adsorption of AP-CQDs on conductive pili will inevitably enhance the EEC of conductive pili, thus promoting the DIET, which was confirmed in the analysis results of the CH4 content (40.82 % for CK and 59.50 % for 0.36 wt% AP-CQDs). The present work provides some indications that a mechanism based on the formation of pili is possible based on the literature and the level of EEC determined in this work. Thirdly, the AP-CQDs may establish a short-distance bridge as a new electron transfer pathway between acetogens and methanogens, as illustrated by Type III in Fig. 5 b. The electrons are quickly transfered over a short distance, which thus promotes the DIET process. Based on the above observations, the AP-CQDs in this work increased the TCOD removal efficiency of the AcoD system from 34.72 to 48.77–57.87 %, the methane content from 40.82 to 50.63–59.50 %, and the cumulative methane production from 201.14 to 266.92–339.64 mL/g VS (184.36 to 244.49–311.14 NmL/g VS), relying on their excellent EEC (Fig. 5 b, Type II and III) and the creation of new electron channels (Fig. 5 b, Type III) to enhance the DIET between acetogens and methanogens. Besides the EEC and the microbial community as the direct evidence in the present work supporting the existed DIET process, in future, the TEM observations on microorganisms, with and without added CQDs, adsorbed on conductive pili of microorganisms enhanced electron transfer between microorganisms, can provide another direct evidence for the existed DIET process in the AD systems, and better illustrate that Type II and III are present.

To further verify the enhanced electron transfer in the AcoD system when AP-CQDs accelerants are added, LSV and CV tests were performed in the CK group and the optimal-dose AP-CQDs group (0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs). As shown in Fig. 6
a, at the same potential (e.g., -0.80 V), the current in the LSV curves for the AcoD system with AP-CQDs (1.02 ± 0.06 mA; absolute value) was significantly (p < 0.05) higher than that in the CK (0.87 ± 0.02 mA; absolute value), indicating that the presence of AP-CQDs increased the electron transfer in the AcoD system. Similar LSV curves were observed in previous works for algae-derived biochar and activated carbon that enhancd electron transfer in the AcoD system (Duan et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2019). In Fig. 6 b, the CV curves revealed that the peak currents of the AcoD system containing AP-CQDs (1.18 ± 0.02 mA) were significantly (p < 0.05) higher than those of the CK (1.12 ± 0.03 mA). This means that the AP-CQDs accelerants promote the redox capacity of the AcoD system. A similar phenomenon is observed in another work, where the introduction of granular activated carbon loaded with nanoscaled zero-valent iron into the AD system obtained more significant redox peaks than the control group (Sun et al., 2021). In general, CV and LSV tests confirm that AP-CQDs accelerants may enhance the electron transfer in Type II and Type III pathways (illustrated in Fig. 5 b), which further strengthen the DIET process. This finding in the CQDs-enhanced AcoD systems is in good accordance with the previous works related to other carbon-based materials (Duan et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2021).

The AP-CQDs accelerants may enhance the electron transfer in our proposed DIET, as confirmed by CV and LSV tests. However, electron transfer occurs between acetogens and methanogens. To confirm the existence of acetogens and methanogens in the AcoD system, microbial community analysis was performed. As illustrated in Fig. 6 c, Proteiniclasticum, Petrimonas, and Sporosarcina were the dominant bacteria in the two samples. Proteiniclasticum and Sporosarcina are mainly responsible for the degradation of organic matter (Lu et al., 2022). Remarkably, the abundance of Proteiniclasticum and Sporosarcina in the 0.36 wt.% group was 39.70 % and 5.41 %, respectively, higher than the CK group (37.56 % and 0.76 %, respectively). This result indicates that the AcoD system with AP-CQDs accelerants has excellent organic matter degradation ability. In addition, Petrimonas belongs to the acetogenes, which can degrade organic matter into acetate and act as electron-donating microorganisms in the DIET process (Yun et al., 2021). It was observed that more Petrimonas were present in the 0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs group (10.40 %) as compared with the CK group (6.27 %). However, the common species, such as Geobacter spp., which participate in DIET were not detected. One possible reason for this finding is that the anaerobic sewage sludge (ASS) used in this study was inoculated via the anaerobic–anoxic–aerobic (A2/O) mode in sewage treatment plant that leads to little or no Geobacter spp. in the ASS. As a result, Geobacter spp. were not detected at the end of AcoD (Gui et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019b). Additionally, Petrimonas were detected as the predominant species, which are involved in the DIET process (Zhao and Zhang, 2019). This finding is an indication that AP-CQDs accelerants enrich the electron-donating microorganisms (acetogens), which may participate in Type II and Type III pathways (Fig. 5 b).

The archaea communities of all samples at the gene level are shown in Fig. 6 d. Methanosarcina and Methanobacterium were the dominant archaea in the two samples. Methanosarcina can produce CH4 through multiple pathways, such as acetotrophic methanogenesis, hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis, and DIET-CO2 reduction (Fig. 5 a), similar to that observed in the previous literature (Wang et al., 2021b). Methanobacterium is hydrogenotrophic methanogen that can only produce CH4 by reducing CO2 with H2 (Mahdy et al., 2019). Notably, the abundance of Methanosarcina and Methanobacterium in the 0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs group (54.20 % and 30.01 %) was significantly increased, higher than in the CK group (31.63 % and 24.38 %), thereby indicating that the AP-CQDs accelerants enrich the electron-accepting microorganisms (methanogens) in Type II and Type III pathways (Fig. 5 b). Bacterial and archaeal community analysis (Fig. 6 c and d) confirmed the existence of electron-donating microorganisms (acetogens, such as Petrimonas) and electron-accepting microorganisms (methanogens, such as Methanosarcina and Methanobacterium), and these microorganisms were enriched in AcoD system containing AP-CQDs. These results further indicate that AP-CQDs may construct the proposed DIET pathways. In addition, future work will perform the microbial analysis by comparing the start-up with final states in all experiments related with the accelerants to better understand and clarify the microbial enrichment.

In conclusion, a general strategy for deciphering the enhanced methanogenic pathway and CH4 production in AcoD systems with the AP-CQDs is illustrated. Firstly, the addition of AP-CQDs can enhance the EEC of the AcoD system and facilitate the DIET process. Secondly, the adsorption of AP-CQDs on conductive pili enhances the electron transfer of conductive pili, thus promoting the DIET process. Thirdly, the AP-CQDs could promote the DIET process by establishing a new electron transfer pathway between acetogens and methanogens. We want to stress that these batch tests are that they can give indication and not real proof of the shifts in populations which need much time to be established, and a continuous anaerobic fermentation technology is suggested to be performed to understand deeply the shifts in the presentation of the results.

Excellent EEC (0.70 μmol e- (g AP-CQDs)-1) of the AP-CQDs significantly improved the AcoD performance. The addition of 0.36 wt.% AP-CQDs to the AcoD system yielded 339.64 mL CH4/g VS and TCOD removal efficiency of 57.87 %. The digestates exhibited superior thermal stability (47.15 %) and potential fertility (46.65 g/kg). The AP-CQDs enriched the electron-donating and electron-accepting microorganisms and promoted the DIET process between acetogens and methanogens. This work provides ideas for improving AcoD performance by using exogenous accelerants and for enhancing the multiple pathways for using AP waste.
